title.zh: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳
title.en: Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu
title.raw: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳 Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu

sentences.zh: 季布，楚人也，為任俠有名。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was a man of Chu; he acted as a knight-errant and was famous for it.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu came from Chu and made a name for himself as a knight-errant who took on obligations for others.

sentences.zh: 項籍使將兵，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji had him lead troops, and he repeatedly put the King of Han in straits.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu employed him as a general, and time and again he cornered the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 項籍滅，高祖購求布千金，敢有舍匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was destroyed, Gaozu offered a thousand catties of gold for Ji Bu; whoever dared harbor or conceal him would be guilty to the third degree of kin.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, the High Ancestor put a bounty of a thousand pounds of gold on Ji Bu’s head, threatening execution of three collateral kin groups for anyone who sheltered him.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，周氏曰：「漢求將軍急，跡且至臣家，能聽臣，臣敢進計；

translations.literal: Bu hid with the Zhou family of Puyang. The Zhou family said: “The Han are urgently seeking the general; their trail will soon reach my house. If you can heed me, I dare advance a plan;
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu took refuge with the Zhou clan in Puyang. The head of the household said: “The court is hunting you relentlessly; their agents will soon trace you here. If you will trust me, I have a plan to propose;

sentences.zh: 即否，願先自剄。」

translations.literal: if not, I wish first to cut my own throat.”
translations.idiomatic: if not, I would rather slit my own throat first.”

sentences.zh: 布許之。

translations.literal: Bu assented.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃髡鉗布，衣褐，置廣柳車中，并與其家僮數十人，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Then they shaved Bu’s head and put an iron collar on him, dressed him in coarse cloth, placed him in a funeral cart, together with several dozen of his household slaves, and went to the home of Zhu Jia of Lu to sell him.
translations.idiomatic: They shaved his head, clamped him in a cangue, dressed him as a convict laborer, hid him in a hearselike wagon with dozens of his household retainers, and sold the whole lot to Zhu Jia of Lu.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知其季布也，買置田舍。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia’s heart knew he was Ji Bu; he bought him and settled him on a farm.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him at once as Ji Bu, bought him, and lodged him on an outlying farm.

sentences.zh: 乃之雒陽見汝陰侯滕公，說曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: Then he went to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Ruoyin, Lord Teng, and urged: “What crime has Ji Bu committed?
translations.idiomatic: He then traveled to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Ruoyin, Xiahou Ying, and argued: “What crime has Ji Bu committed?

sentences.zh: 臣各為其主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each served their own lord in use; it was duty, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers each served their own master—that was simply their duty.

sentences.zh: 項氏臣豈可盡誅邪？

translations.literal: Can all of Xiang’s ministers be executed to the last?
translations.idiomatic: Are we to execute every minister who once served Xiang Yu?

sentences.zh: 今上始得天下，而以私怨求一人，何示不廣也！

translations.literal: Now the sovereign has just obtained the empire, yet for a private grudge seeks one man—what a narrow signal that sends!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven has only just won the realm; to hunt one man out of personal spite shows how little largeness of mind he displays.

sentences.zh: 且以季布之賢，漢求之急如此，此不北走胡，南走越耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, with Ji Bu’s worthiness, the Han seeking him so urgently—this is nothing but fleeing north to the Hu or south to Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, a man of Ji Bu’s caliber, pressed this hard, will flee north to the Xiongnu or south to Nanyue.

sentences.zh: 夫忌壯士以資敵國，此伍子胥所以鞭荊平之墓也。

translations.literal: To drive a brave man into the arms of an enemy state—this is why Wu Zixu whipped King Ping of Jing’s grave.
translations.idiomatic: Hounding a stalwart into the service of a rival—that is how Wu Zixu came to flog the tomb of King Ping of Chu.

sentences.zh: 君何不從容為上言之？」

translations.literal: Why does my lord not at ease speak to the sovereign about this?”
translations.idiomatic: Why will you not find a tactful moment to speak to the emperor on his behalf?”

sentences.zh: 滕公心知朱家大俠，意布匿其所，乃許諾。

translations.literal: Lord Teng’s heart knew Zhu Jia was a great knight-errant; his meaning was that Bu was hidden with him; then he promised.
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Ying knew Zhu Jia was a famous knight-errant and inferred that Ji Bu lay concealed with him; he agreed to help.

sentences.zh: 侍間，果言如朱家指。

translations.literal: Waiting his opportunity in attendance, he indeed spoke as Zhu Jia had directed.
translations.idiomatic: When a suitable moment arose at court, he repeated Zhu Jia’s argument to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上乃赦布。

translations.literal: The sovereign then pardoned Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor thereupon pardoned Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 當是時，諸公皆多布能摧剛為柔，朱家亦以此名聞當世。

translations.literal: At that time, the great men all praised Bu for being able to grind down the hard into the soft; Zhu Jia also by this had his name heard in the age.
translations.idiomatic: Men of rank admired Ji Bu for turning inflexible pride into yielding survival; Zhu Jia’s reputation spread across the age for the same deed.

sentences.zh: 布召見，謝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Bu was summoned to audience, thanked him, and was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was summoned, expressed his gratitude, and was appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為中郎將。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Hui, he was general of the gentlemen of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui he rose to general of the gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 單于嘗為書嫚呂太后，太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu once sent a letter insulting Empress Dowager Lü; the empress dowager was angry and summoned the generals to deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu had once sent a letter that grossly insulted Empress Dowager Lü; enraged, she called the generals to council.

sentences.zh: 上將軍樊噲曰：「臣願得十萬眾，橫行匈奴中。」

translations.literal: The senior general Fan Kuai said: “Your servant would like to obtain a hundred thousand men and sweep through the midst of the Xiongnu.”
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai, chief among the generals, said: “Give me a hundred thousand troops and I will ride roughshod through the Xiongnu heartland.”

sentences.zh: 諸將皆阿呂太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: The generals all fawned on Empress Dowager Lü and took Kuai’s words as right.
translations.idiomatic: The other generals toadied to the empress dowager and echoed Fan Kuai’s bravado.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也。

translations.literal: Bu said: “Fan Kuai ought to be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu said: “Fan Kuai deserves execution.

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十餘萬，困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: For with the High Ancestor’s army of more than three hundred thousand men trapped at Pingcheng, Kuai was then also among them.
translations.idiomatic: When the High Ancestor’s three hundred thousand–strong host was trapped at Pingcheng, Fan Kuai was in that very army.

sentences.zh: 今噲奈何以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，面謾！

translations.literal: Now how can Kuai presume, with a hundred thousand men, to sweep through the midst of the Xiongnu—this is deceit to your face!
translations.idiomatic: How dare he now boast that a mere hundred thousand can overrun the steppe? That is barefaced deceit before the court.

sentences.zh: 且秦以事胡，陳勝等起。

translations.literal: Moreover, Qin because of affairs with the Hu, Chen Sheng and others rose.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, the Qin dynasty exhausted itself on the northern frontier, and Chen Sheng and his like rose in rebellion.

sentences.zh: 今瘡痍未瘳，噲又面諛，欲搖動天下。」

translations.literal: Now the wounds are not yet healed, and Kuai again flatters to your face, wishing to shake the realm.”
translations.idiomatic: The realm is still bleeding from those wars, and Fan Kuai compounds it with sycophantic flattery that would unsettle the whole empire.”

sentences.zh: 是時殿上皆恐，太后罷朝，遂不復議擊匈奴事。

translations.literal: At that time those on the dais were all afraid; the empress dowager dismissed court, and thereafter did not again deliberate attacking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The hall fell silent with fear; the empress dowager ended the session and never again discussed striking the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 布為河東守。

translations.literal: Bu was governor of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: He was appointed governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，人有言其賢，召欲以為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Wen, someone spoke of his worthiness; he was summoned with intent to make him imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen, someone praised his talent, and the court summoned him intending to name him imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 人又言其勇，使酒難近。

translations.literal: Someone again spoke of his courage, that he was given to wine and hard to approach.
translations.idiomatic: Others then spoke of his rash courage and fondness for drink, calling him unapproachable.

sentences.zh: 至，留邸一月，見罷。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was lodged in the guesthouse a month, then dismissed from consideration.
translations.idiomatic: He waited a month in the official hostel and was sent home without appointment.

sentences.zh: 布進曰：「臣待罪河東，陛下無故召臣，此人必有以臣欺陛下者。

translations.literal: Bu advanced and said: “Your servant held office awaiting punishment at Hedong; for no reason Your Majesty summoned me—this means someone must have used me to deceive Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu stepped forward: “I was serving at my post in Hedong when you summoned me without cause—someone must have oversold me to you.

sentences.zh: 今臣至，無所受事，罷去，此人必有毀臣者。

translations.literal: Now your servant has arrived, received no assignment, and is dismissed to go—this means someone must have slandered me.
translations.idiomatic: I have come, been given no charge, and am dismissed—someone must have blackened my name.

sentences.zh: 夫陛下以一人譽召臣，一人毀去臣，臣恐天下有識者聞之，有以窺陛下。」

translations.literal: For Your Majesty to summon your servant on one man’s praise and dismiss your servant on one man’s slander—your servant fears that men of insight in the realm, hearing this, will thereby peer into Your Majesty.”
translations.idiomatic: To elevate me on one man’s word and cast me aside on another’s will let the discerning read your intentions all too clearly.”

sentences.zh: 上默然，慚曰：「河東吾股肱郡，故特召君耳。」

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent, ashamed, and said: “Hedong is my arm-and-thigh commandery; therefore I especially summoned you.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sat in silence, then said with embarrassment: “Hedong is a pillar province of mine; I merely wished to see you.”

sentences.zh: 布之官。

translations.literal: Bu returned to his post.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu returned to his duties in Hedong.

sentences.zh: 辯士曹丘生數招權顧金錢，事貴人趙談等，與竇長君善。

translations.literal: The rhetorician Cao Qiusheng repeatedly wielded influence and eyed money; he served the powerful such as Zhao Tan and was on good terms with the elder Lord Dou.
translations.idiomatic: The persuader Cao Qiusheng traded on connections for profit, curried favor with grandees like Zhao Tan, and was close to Dou Zhang, the empress’s elder brother.

sentences.zh: 布聞，寄書諫長君曰：「吾聞曹丘生非長者，勿與通。」

translations.literal: Bu heard of it and sent a letter urging the elder lord: “I have heard Cao Qiusheng is not a man of integrity; do not associate with him.”
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu heard of it and wrote warning Dou Zhang: “Cao Qiusheng is no gentleman; keep your distance.”

sentences.zh: 及曹丘生歸，欲得書請布。

translations.literal: When Cao Qiusheng returned, he wished to obtain a letter to request Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng came back, he wanted a letter of introduction to Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 竇長君曰：「季將軍不說足下，足下無往。」

translations.literal: The elder Lord Dou said: “General Ji is not pleased with you; you must not go.”
translations.idiomatic: Dou Zhang said: “General Ji dislikes you; do not go.”

sentences.zh: 固請書，遂行。

translations.literal: He firmly requested a letter, then set out.
translations.idiomatic: Cao pressed until he got the letter and set off.

sentences.zh: 使人先發書，布果大怒，待曹丘。

translations.literal: He had a man go ahead with the letter; Bu was indeed greatly angry and awaited Cao Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: He sent a messenger ahead with the missive; Ji Bu was furious and waited for him.

sentences.zh: 曹丘至，則揖布曰：「楚人諺曰『得黃金百，不如得季布諾』，足下何以得此聲梁楚之間哉？

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu arrived, he bowed to Bu and said: “The men of Chu have a proverb: ‘Better gain a hundred catties of yellow gold than gain Ji Bu’s promise’—how did you obtain this reputation between Liang and Chu?
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng arrived, he bowed and said: “Chu has a saying: ‘A hundred pounds of gold cannot match Ji Bu’s word’—how did you earn such fame from Liang to Chu?

sentences.zh: 且僕與足下俱楚人，使僕游揚足下名於天下，顧不美乎？

translations.literal: Moreover, your servant and you are both men of Chu; were your servant to spread your name through the realm, would it not be fine?
translations.idiomatic: We are both sons of Chu; were I to trumpet your name across the empire, would that not be splendid?

sentences.zh: 何足下距僕之深也！」

translations.literal: Why do you, sir, keep me at such a distance!”
translations.idiomatic: Why do you shut me out so coldly!”

sentences.zh: 布乃大說。

translations.literal: Bu was then greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu’s anger melted into delight.

sentences.zh: 引入，留數月，為上客，厚送之。

translations.literal: He led him in, kept him several months, treated him as a honored guest, and sent him off generously.
translations.idiomatic: He brought him inside, kept him for months as an honored guest, and sent him away laden with gifts.

sentences.zh: 布名所以益聞者，曹丘揚之也。

translations.literal: The reason Bu’s name became still more heard was that Cao Qiu spread it.
translations.idiomatic: It was Cao Qiusheng’s praise that made Ji Bu’s name resound still farther.

sentences.zh: 布弟季心氣蓋關中，遇人恭謹，為任俠，方數千里，士爭為死。

translations.literal: Bu’s younger brother Ji Xin’s spirit covered Guanzhong; encountering people he was respectful and careful; acting as knight-errant, within several thousand li, gentlemen strove to die for him.
translations.idiomatic: His younger brother Ji Xin’s reputation overshadowed Guanzhong: courteous in person, he lived the knight-errant’s code, and for a thousand miles around men vied to die for him.

sentences.zh: 嘗殺人，亡吳，從爰絲匿，長事爰絲，弟畜灌夫、籍福之屬。

translations.literal: He once killed a man and fled to Wu, followed Yuan Ang into hiding, served Yuan Ang as an elder brother, and treated Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their like as younger brothers.
translations.idiomatic: After killing a man he fled to Wu, hid with Yuan Ang, honored Yuan as an elder brother, and treated Guan Fu and Ji Fu as juniors.

sentences.zh: 嘗為中司馬，中尉郅都不敢加。

translations.literal: He once was marshal of the center; Central Commandant Zhi Du did not dare touch him.
translations.idiomatic: As marshal of the center guard he was untouchable—even Commandant Zhi Du would not cross him.

sentences.zh: 少年多時時竊借其名以行。

translations.literal: Young men often secretly borrowed his name to act.
translations.idiomatic: Youths constantly invoked his name without permission to throw their weight around.

sentences.zh: 當是時，季心以勇，布以諾，聞關中。

translations.literal: At that time, Ji Xin by courage, Bu by keeping his word, were heard of in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Guanzhong knew Ji Xin for daring and Ji Bu for keeping his promises.

sentences.zh: 布母弟丁公，為項羽將，逐窘高祖彭城西。

translations.literal: Bu’s mother’s younger brother Ding Gong was a general for Xiang Yu; he pursued and cornered the High Ancestor west of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu’s maternal uncle Ding Gong served Xiang Yu as a general and once had the High Ancestor hemmed in west of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 短兵接，漢王急，顧謂丁公曰：「兩賢豈相厄哉！」

translations.literal: With short weapons engaged, the King of Han was in straits; he looked back and said to Ding Gong: “Are two worthy men to harm each other!”
translations.idiomatic: In close combat the King of Han was desperate; glancing back he cried to Ding Gong: “Do two good men really have to destroy each other?”

sentences.zh: 丁公引兵而還。

translations.literal: Ding Gong led his troops and withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Gong broke off the pursuit and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 及項王滅，丁公謁見高祖，以丁公徇軍中，曰：「丁公為項王臣不忠，使項王失天下者也。」

translations.literal: When King Xiang was destroyed, Ding Gong went to audience with the High Ancestor; taking Ding Gong, he displayed him in the army, saying: “Ding Gong was Xiang Yu’s minister and disloyal; he is the one who made King Xiang lose the realm.”
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Ding Gong presented himself to the founder, who had him paraded before the army, declaring: “This man was Xiang Yu’s minister yet betrayed him; he is why Xiang Yu lost the empire.”

sentences.zh: 遂斬之，曰：「使後為人臣無傚丁公也！」

translations.literal: Thereupon he beheaded him, saying: “Let later men who serve as ministers not imitate Ding Gong!”
translations.idiomatic: He then beheaded him, adding: “Let no future minister take Ding Gong as a model!”

sentences.zh: 欒布，梁人也。

translations.literal: Luan Bu was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was a native of Liang.

sentences.zh: 彭越為家人時，嘗與布游，窮困，賣庸於齊，為酒家保。

translations.literal: When Peng Yue was still a commoner, he once roamed with Bu; in poverty they sold labor in Qi, serving as a tavern bailiff.
translations.idiomatic: When Peng Yue was a commoner, he and Luan Bu traveled together; broke, they hired out as laborers in Qi, with Luan tending a wineshop.

sentences.zh: 數歲別去，而布為人所略，賣為奴於燕。

translations.literal: After several years they parted; Bu was kidnapped by someone and sold as a slave in Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Years later they separated; kidnappers sold Luan Bu into slavery in Yan.

sentences.zh: 為其主家報仇，燕將臧荼舉以為都尉。

translations.literal: For his master’s household he avenged a wrong; the Yan general Zang Tu raised him and made him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He avenged his master’s household; the Yan general Zang Tu promoted him to commandant.

sentences.zh: 荼為燕王，布為將。

translations.literal: When Tu became King of Yan, Bu was a general.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu became king of Yan, Luan Bu served as his general.

sentences.zh: 及荼反，漢擊燕，虜布。

translations.literal: When Tu rebelled, the Han struck Yan and captured Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu rebelled, Han forces attacked Yan and took Luan Bu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 梁王彭越聞之，乃言上，請贖布為梁大夫。

translations.literal: King Liang Peng Yue heard of it, then spoke to the sovereign, requesting to ransom Bu as a grandee of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: King Peng Yue of Liang petitioned the throne to ransom him as a Liang grandee.

sentences.zh: 使於齊，未反，漢召彭越責以謀反，夷三族，梟首雒陽，下詔有收視者輒捕之。

translations.literal: Sent on mission to Qi, before he returned, the Han summoned Peng Yue and charged him with plotting rebellion, exterminated three degrees of kin, displayed the head at Luoyang, and issued an edict that whoever received or viewed the corpse would be arrested at once.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was on a mission to Qi when the court summoned Peng Yue, convicted him of treason, wiped out three kin groups, exposed Peng’s head in Luoyang, and decreed death for anyone who dared mourn or view the body.

sentences.zh: 布還，奏事彭越頭下，祠而哭之。

translations.literal: Bu returned, reported his business beneath Peng Yue’s head, sacrificed, and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu returned, made his report beneath Peng Yue’s severed head, offered sacrifice, and wept.

sentences.zh: 吏捕以聞。

translations.literal: The clerks arrested him and reported it.
translations.idiomatic: Officials seized him and reported the matter.

sentences.zh: 上召布罵曰：「若與彭越反邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned Bu and reviled: “Did you conspire with Peng Yue to rebel?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor hauled him in and snarled: “Did you plot rebellion with Peng Yue?

sentences.zh: 吾禁人勿收，若獨祠而哭之，與反明矣。

translations.literal: I forbade men to receive him; you alone sacrificed and wept for him—with rebellion it is clear.”
translations.idiomatic: I forbade anyone to mourn him; you alone wept over him—that proves your guilt.”

sentences.zh: 趣亨之。」

translations.literal: Hurry and boil him.”
translations.idiomatic: Boil him at once.”

sentences.zh: 方提趨湯，顧曰：「願一言而死。」

translations.literal: Just as he was being led toward the cauldron, he looked back and said: “I wish one word before I die.”
translations.idiomatic: As guards marched him toward the boiling pot, he turned and said: “Let me speak one sentence before I die.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何言？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: “What words?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked: “What is it?”

sentences.zh: 布曰：「方上之困彭城，敗滎陽、成皋間，項王所以不能遂西，徒以彭王居梁地，與漢合從苦楚也。

translations.literal: Bu said: “When Your Majesty was trapped at Pengcheng, defeated between Xingyang and Chenggao, the reason King Xiang could not press west was solely because King Peng held Liang territory and allied with Han to exhaust Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu said: “When you were trapped at Pengcheng and routed between Xingyang and Chenggao, the only reason Xiang Yu could not drive west was that Peng Yue held the Liang region in alliance with Han and wore Chu down.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，彭王壹顧，與楚則漢破，與漢則楚破。

translations.literal: At that time, had King Peng turned his glance once—with Chu then Han would have broken; with Han then Chu would have broken.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment a single shift of Peng Yue’s loyalty would have shattered either Han or Chu.

sentences.zh: 且垓下之會，微彭王，項氏不亡。

translations.literal: Moreover, at the meeting below Gaixia, without King Peng, the Xiang clan would not have perished.
translations.idiomatic: At Gaixia, without Peng Yue, Xiang Yu would never have fallen.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，彭王剖符受封，亦欲傳之萬世。

translations.literal: When the realm was settled, King Peng received the tally and investiture, yet also wished to pass them on for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: Peace won, Peng Yue took his bronze tally and fief, expecting them to pass to endless heirs.

sentences.zh: 今漢壹徵兵於梁，彭王病不行，而疑以為反。

translations.literal: Now the Han once conscripted troops in Liang; King Peng was ill and did not go, and on suspicion was deemed to rebel.
translations.idiomatic: Now a single levy in Liang found him ill and unable to march, and you branded him a traitor on suspicion alone.

sentences.zh: 反形未見，以苛細誅之，臣恐功臣人人自危也。

translations.literal: With no sign of rebellion visible, yet executed on petty pretext—your servant fears that merit-holders will each fear for himself.
translations.idiomatic: No rebellion showed itself, yet you killed him on trifling charges—every meritorious minister must now tremble for his life.

sentences.zh: 今彭王已死，臣生不如死，請就亨。」

translations.literal: Now King Peng is dead; your servant to live is not as good as to die; I request to submit to boiling.”
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue is dead; for me to live would be worse than death—send me to the cauldron.”

sentences.zh: 上乃釋布，拜為都尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign then released Bu and appointed him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him and named him commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，為燕相，至將軍。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Wen, he was chancellor of Yan, reaching general.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen he served as chancellor of Yan and rose to general.

sentences.zh: 布稱曰：「窮困不能辱身，非人也；

translations.literal: Bu declared: “In poverty unable to humble the body is not to be human;
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu declared: “To endure want without debasing oneself is less than human;

sentences.zh: 富貴不能快意，非賢也。」

translations.literal: in wealth unable to satisfy the will is not to be worthy.”
translations.idiomatic: to enjoy riches without acting on one’s convictions is less than noble.”

sentences.zh: 於是嘗有德，厚報之；

translations.literal: Thereupon toward those who had shown him kindness, he repaid them richly;
translations.idiomatic: Whenever someone had done him a kindness, he repaid it many times over.

sentences.zh: 有怨，必以法滅之。

translations.literal: where he had enmity, he was sure to destroy them by means of the law.
translations.idiomatic: If anyone had earned his hatred, he saw to it that the law brought them down.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，以功封為鄃侯，復為燕相。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, by merit he was enfeoffed as marquis of Yu, again chancellor of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: When the Wu–Chu rebellion broke out, his service earned him the marquisate of Yu and a second term as Yan’s chancellor.

sentences.zh: 燕齊之間皆為立社，號曰欒公社。

translations.literal: Between Yan and Qi they all established shrines for him, styled the shrines of Lord Luan.
translations.idiomatic: From Yan to Qi the people built shrines to him, calling them the altars of Lord Luan.

sentences.zh: 布薨，子賁嗣侯，孝武時坐為太常犧牲不如令，國除。

translations.literal: Bu died; his son Ben inherited the marquisate; in the time of Emperor Wu he sat guilty because as grand master of ceremonies the sacrificial victims did not accord with regulations, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu died; his son Luan Ben inherited the title, but under Emperor Wu lost the fief when, as grand master of ceremonies, he mishandled sacrificial victims.

sentences.zh: 田叔，趙陘城人也。

translations.literal: Tian Shu was a man of Xingcheng in Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu came from Xingcheng in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 其先，齊田氏也。

translations.literal: His ancestors were the Tian clan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears were the Tian lineage of Qi.

sentences.zh: 叔好劍，學黃老術於樂鉅公。

translations.literal: Shu loved the sword and studied Huang–Lao techniques with Lord Yue Ju.
translations.idiomatic: He loved swordsmanship and studied Huang–Lao doctrine under Yue Ju.

sentences.zh: 為人廉直，喜任俠。

translations.literal: As a man he was incorrupt and straight, fond of acting as knight-errant.
translations.idiomatic: Upright and incorruptible, he too lived by the knight-errant code.

sentences.zh: 游諸公，趙人舉之趙相趙午，言之趙王張敖，以為郎中。

translations.literal: He roamed among the great men; men of Zhao recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu of Zhao, who spoke to King Zhang Ao of Zhao, and he was made gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He moved among the powerful of Zhao; they recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu, who introduced him to King Zhang Ao, who appointed him a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 數歲，趙王賢之，未及遷。

translations.literal: After several years the king of Zhao deemed him worthy but had not yet promoted him.
translations.idiomatic: For years the king admired him but had not yet advanced his rank.

sentences.zh: 會趙午、貫高等謀弒上，事發覺，漢下詔捕趙王及群臣反者。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted to assassinate the sovereign; the affair was discovered; the Han issued an edict to arrest the king of Zhao and the ministers who had rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted regicide; when the plot surfaced, Han issued warrants for King Zhang Ao and every minister implicated.

sentences.zh: 趙有敢隨王，罪三族。

translations.literal: In Zhao whoever dared follow the king would be guilty to the third degree of kin.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhao anyone who dared stay with the king faced execution to the third degree of kin.

sentences.zh: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。

translations.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and more than ten others put on crimson convict garb, shaved their own heads, and clamped their own cangues, and followed the king to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen others donned the convict's russet, shaved their heads, and locked on the cangue of their own accord, then followed their king to the capital.

sentences.zh: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。

translations.literal: When the affair of King Ao of Zhao was cleared, he was released; the king was demoted to Marquis of Xuanping, and he then recommended Shu and the ten men upward.
translations.idiomatic: Once King Zhang Ao was cleared, he went free and was reduced to Marquis of Xuanping; he then urged the throne to employ Tian Shu and the others.

sentences.zh: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned them to audience and spoke with them; among the ministers at the Han court none could surpass them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received them and talked with them; no minister at court could match their quality.

sentences.zh: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。

translations.literal: The sovereign was pleased and appointed them all grand administrators or chancellors of the feudal kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: Pleased, he named each of them either a commandery governor or a kingdom chancellor.

sentences.zh: 叔為漢中守十餘年。

translations.literal: Shu was grand administrator of Hanzhong for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for over a decade.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen had just ascended the throne, he summoned Shu and asked: "Do you know who are the true elders of the realm?"
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Wen's reign he called Tian Shu in and asked: "Who in the empire still embodies old-fashioned integrity?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」

translations.literal: He replied: "How could your servant be fit to know such men!"
translations.idiomatic: He answered: "I am hardly worthy to judge that."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "You, sir, are an elder; you ought to know."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said: "You are such a man yourself; you must have an opinion."

sentences.zh: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: "The former grand administrator of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is an elder."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and named Meng Shu, the former governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。

translations.literal: At that time Meng Shu had been dismissed because the barbarians had entered Yunzhong in great force.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just lost his post after a major Xiongnu raid on Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "The late emperor stationed Meng Shu in Yunzhong for more than ten years; when the barbarians once entered, Meng Shu could not hold firm; for no good reason several hundred soldiers died in battle.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor objected: "The late emperor kept Meng Shu in Yunzhong for years; one raid broke through, he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of men died for nothing."

sentences.zh: 長者固殺人乎？」

translations.literal: Do elders by nature kill people?"
translations.idiomatic: Is that what you call a man of principle—to get soldiers killed?"

sentences.zh: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: "As for Guan Gao and the others plotting rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued an enlightened edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty to the third degree of kin; yet Meng Shu shaved his head and clamped a cangue on himself and followed King Zhang, intending to die for him—how could he have known he would become grand administrator of Yunzhong!
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed again: "When Guan Gao plotted treason, Your Majesty decreed that anyone in Zhao who stayed with King Zhang would die with three collateral lines—yet Meng Shu disfigured himself like a convict and followed his lord, ready to perish with him. He could not have foreseen he would ever govern Yunzhong."

sentences.zh: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！

translations.literal: Han and Chu faced each other; the soldiers were worn out, while Modu of the Xiongnu had newly brought the northern tribes to heel and came raiding the border; Meng Shu knew the troops were exhausted and could not bear to order them out; the men vied to man the walls and die before the foe like sons for a father—therefore several hundred died—how could Meng Shu have driven them to it!
translations.idiomatic: Han and Chu had bled each other white, Modu had just united the steppe, and raiders poured against the frontier. Meng Shu saw his men were spent and would not drive them; they stormed the walls and died like sons defending a father—not because he whipped them forward.

sentences.zh: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」

translations.literal: This is why Meng Shu counts as an elder."
translations.idiomatic: That is the mark of a true elder."

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said: "How worthy is Meng Shu!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed: "Meng Shu is a worthy man!"

sentences.zh: 復召以為雲中守。

translations.literal: He was summoned again and made grand administrator of Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu was recalled and reappointed governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。

translations.literal: After several years Shu by law lost his office.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later Tian Shu lost his post through a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Liang had someone murder the Han counsellor Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang, and he obtained the full facts.
translations.idiomatic: When King Liu Wu of Liang had Yuan Ang assassinated, Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to probe the kingdom; he uncovered the whole plot.

sentences.zh: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」

translations.literal: Returning to report, the sovereign said: "Did Liang do it?"
translations.idiomatic: On his return the emperor asked point-blank: "Was Liang behind it?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有之。」

translations.literal: He replied: "There is such a thing."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered: "Yes."

sentences.zh: 「事安在？」

translations.literal: "Where is the affair?"
translations.idiomatic: "Where is the proof?"

sentences.zh: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。

translations.literal: Shu said: "Your Majesty had best not press the matter of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu said: "You should not pursue this further."

sentences.zh: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；

translations.literal: If now the King of Liang is not put to death, that is casting aside Han law;
translations.idiomatic: If the king of Liang goes unpunished, the law of Han is empty;

sentences.zh: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」

translations.literal: if he is put to death, the empress dowager will find no sweetness in her food and no rest on her mat—this worry will lie on Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: if he is executed, the empress dowager will neither eat nor sleep in peace—and the burden will be yours."

sentences.zh: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his candor and named him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.zh: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his candor and named him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.zh: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？

translations.literal: When the chancellor first arrived at his post, the people who on their own account spoke because the king had seized their property were more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after he took office, well over a hundred commoners petitioned that the king had seized their goods.

sentences.zh: 何敢自言主！」

translations.literal: Shu took their ringleaders—two thousand men—and had them flogged, angrily saying to them: "Is the king not your lord? How dare you speak against your lord yourselves!"
translations.idiomatic: He seized some two thousand ringleaders among them, had them flogged, and thundered: "The king is your sovereign—how dare you denounce him in open court!"

sentences.zh: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。

translations.literal: The King of Lu heard of it, was greatly ashamed, opened the middle treasury, and had the chancellor compensate them.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu, mortified, opened his privy purse and told the chancellor to repay the people.

sentences.zh: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」

translations.literal: The chancellor said: "Let the king himself send men to repay them; if not so, this is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu refused: "Your Highness must make the restitution yourself; otherwise the people will praise your minister and blame their prince."

sentences.zh: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。

translations.literal: The King of Lu loved hunting; the chancellor often followed him into the park; the king always rested the chancellor by sending him to the lodge.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; Tian Shu always rode into the preserve with him, but the king kept sending him back to the guesthouse to rest.

sentences.zh: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」

translations.literal: The chancellor often sat exposed outside the park, to the end not resting, saying: "Our king is exposed to wind and sun; why alone should I lodge?"
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu instead sat in the open beyond the wall, refusing to leave, saying: "If my lord endures the weather, how may I hide indoors?"

sentences.zh: 王以故不大出遊。

translations.literal: For this reason the king did not much go out on excursions.
translations.idiomatic: The king therefore hunted far less often.

sentences.zh: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」

translations.literal: After several years he died in office; Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for sacrifice; the youngest son Ren did not accept, saying: "By righteousness I do not harm the prior man's name."
translations.idiomatic: He died in harness a few years later. Lu offered his family a hundred pounds of gold for funeral rites; his youngest son Tian Ren declined, saying: "Accepting would tarnish my father's reputation."

sentences.zh: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Ren by strength and courage became a retainer of the general-in-chief Wei; several times he followed to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Ren, bold and hardy, became a client of General-in-chief Wei Qing and repeatedly campaigned against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。

translations.literal: The general-in-chief advanced words recommending Ren as gentleman of the palace; he reached two thousand piculs, chief clerk of the chancellor, then lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing recommended him as a gentleman of the palace; he rose to two thousand piculs and chief clerk under the chancellor before losing his post.

sentences.zh: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。

translations.literal: Later he was sent to investigate the Three Rivers region; returning, his memorials pleased the throne, and he was appointed metropolitan area commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later, as an inspector of the Three He commanderies, he filed reports that satisfied the emperor and was named capital-area commandant.

sentences.zh: 月餘，遷司直。

translations.literal: After more than a month he was promoted to metropolitan clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Within a month he advanced to metropolitan clerk.

sentences.zh: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。

translations.literal: After several years the heir apparent Li raised troops; Ren's section closed the city gates yet let the heir apparent escape; he sat guilty of releasing a rebel and his kin were exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: Years afterward, when Prince Li mutiny broke out, Tian Ren's unit barred the gates but allowed the heir to flee; he was convicted of abetting rebellion and his whole clan was executed.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。

translations.literal: The appraiser says: With Xiang Yu's heroic temper, Ji Bu nonetheless won fame in Chu for courage; he repeatedly entered battle and tore enemy banners—he may be called a stalwart.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Even under Xiang Yu's towering pride, Ji Bu made a name in Chu for valor, time and again seizing standards in the melee—a true fighting man.

sentences.zh: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？

translations.literal: As for being trapped in disgrace and slavery, clinging to life without altering—why?
translations.idiomatic: Why, then, did he endure the shame of chains and servitude rather than die?

sentences.zh: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。

translations.literal: They themselves trusted their abilities; receiving insult without feeling it shameful, they wished still to be of use before their talents were spent—therefore in the end they became famous generals of Han.
translations.idiomatic: They trusted their gifts: humiliation meant little if they could yet serve; only thus did they become celebrated Han generals.

sentences.zh: 賢者誠重其死。

translations.literal: Worthy men truly weigh how they meet their death.
translations.idiomatic: Men of worth take death seriously.

sentences.zh: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。

translations.literal: Maids and concubines of low station, stirred to indignation and killing themselves—this is not true courage; their scheming is merely the shallowest sort.
translations.idiomatic: Bondmaids who stab themselves in a fit of pique show no bravery—only the pettiest spite.

sentences.zh: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！

translations.literal: Luan Bu weeping for Peng Yue, Tian Shu following Zhang Ao, going to death as if going home—they truly knew their place; though set beside the martyrs of old, how could they be surpassed!
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourning Peng Yue, Tian Shu cleaving to Zhang Ao—they marched toward execution as toward a feast, knowing exactly where they stood; the ancient paragons of fidelity could do no more.
